ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic cookie class; no specific gingerbread cookie term exists. Broader class used as the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A003KZ covers the biscuits and cookies category; no gingerbread-specific code exists. Broader category code used as the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for gingerbread cookie; left null to maintain ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard gingerbread cookie weighs approximately 28 g, providing around 130 kcal, 22 g carbohydrate, and 13 g sugar per serving.

nutrient_source.title: Cookies, gingersnaps — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 18159)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for gingersnap cookies used as the primary reference for saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, potassium, and cholesterol. Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre) are sourced directly from the Singapore HPB Foodo database (cr_id F11080016, confidence 0.95). Calcium and iron are reported as 0.0 in the HPB source; USDA values used for cross-reference on micronutrients where HPB reports zero.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for gingerbread cookie was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. The GI value of 56 is estimated from the closest analogous entry: plain sweet biscuits/cookies (GI ~55–57, medium category) as reported in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI × carbohydrate per serving (g) / 100 = 56 × 22.0 / 100 = 12.3. Input record cited GI 55 from an 'Embedded GI Database' (confidence 0.45, non-approved source); value revised to 56 from the approved analogous estimate. GL per serving based on 28 g serving size providing 22.0 g available carbohydrate.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sugar (46.4 g/100 g) with zero dietary fibre drives insulin-mediated sodium retention; sodium at 321 mg/100 g is borderline elevated; saturated fat at 3.1 g/100 g and trans fat at 0.1 g/100 g contribute to vascular stiffness.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid gingerbread cookies due to the combination of extremely high sugar content promoting insulin-driven sodium retention, borderline sodium load, and the presence of trans fat; even occasional consumption of small portions is inadvisable for those with poorly controlled blood pressure.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar at 46.4 g/100 g far exceeds the 20 g/100 g glycaemic ceiling; total carbohydrate is 78.6 g/100 g with zero dietary fibre to moderate absorption, producing rapid and severe postprandial blood glucose elevation; GI is medium (56) but glycemic load per serving is significant at 12.3.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Gingerbread cookies are contraindicated for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to critically high sugar and refined carbohydrate content with no fibre buffer; even a single cookie (28 g) delivers approximately 13 g of sugar and 22 g of rapidly absorbed carbohydrate, causing sharp postprandial glucose spikes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Trans fat (0.1 g/100 g) raises LDL and lowers HDL cholesterol; saturated fat (3.1 g/100 g) contributes to atherogenic lipid profile; very high sugar (46.4 g/100 g) promotes hypertriglyceridaemia and endothelial inflammation.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid gingerbread cookies as the combination of trans fat, saturated fat, and very high refined sugar collectively promotes atherogenesis, hypertriglyceridaemia, and systemic inflammation, all of which are independent risk factors for adverse cardiovascular events.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Refined sugar (46.4 g/100 g) promotes pro-inflammatory cytokine production; trans fat (0.1 g/100 g) is strongly pro-inflammatory; despite the presence of ginger and cinnamon, their quantities in a commercial cookie are insufficient to confer meaningful anti-inflammatory benefit.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid gingerbread cookies as the high refined sugar and trans fat content outweigh any marginal anti-inflammatory benefit from trace spice ingredients, and regular consumption is likely to exacerbate systemic inflammation and joint symptoms.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Gingerbread cookie is a baked sweet biscuit of Western origin, traditionally associated with Northern European and North American culinary traditions. It is made from wheat flour, molasses, refined sugar, butter or shortening, and a characteristic blend of warm spices including ground ginger, cinnamon, and nutmeg, leavened with baking soda and bound with egg. The texture ranges from soft and chewy to crisp and snappy depending on the recipe and baking time, with a deep brown colour derived from molasses and a distinctively warm, spiced sweetness. Nutritionally, gingerbread cookies are energy-dense at 464 kcal per 100 g, with a very high carbohydrate content of 78.6 g/100 g of which 46.4 g is sugar, and a moderate fat content of 12.5 g/100 g. Dietary fibre is absent (0.0 g/100 g), meaning there is no buffer to moderate the rapid absorption of refined carbohydrates. Sodium is 321 mg/100 g, which is borderline elevated for a sweet snack. The glycemic index is estimated at 56 (medium category), but the glycemic load per standard 28 g serving is approximately 12.3, reflecting a meaningful postprandial glucose impact. Clinically, gingerbread cookies are unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to critically high sugar and rapidly absorbed carbohydrate with no fibre modulation. They are also contraindicated for hypertension due to the combination of high sugar driving insulin-mediated sodium retention and borderline sodium content. For cardiovascular disease, the presence of trans fat and saturated fat alongside very high sugar makes this food a high-concern item. Despite the anti-inflammatory reputation of ginger and cinnamon, the quantities present in a commercial cookie are insufficient to offset the pro-inflammatory effects of high refined sugar and trans fat. Gingerbread cookies are best regarded as an occasional indulgence for healthy individuals and should be avoided by those managing chronic metabolic or cardiovascular conditions.
common_user_queries: How much sugar is in a gingerbread cookie?
Is gingerbread cookie high in calories?
Can I eat gingerbread cookies if I have high blood pressure?
What is the glycemic index of gingerbread cookie?
Is gingerbread cookie suitable for heart disease patients?
How many carbs in a gingerbread cookie?
What spices are in gingerbread cookie?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from Singapore HPB Foodo database (cr_id F11080016, confidence 0.95, reviewed status). Secondary nutrient values (saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, potassium, cholesterol) cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for gingersnap cookies (NDB 18159), which is the closest approved-source equivalent. GI estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry for plain sweet biscuits/cookies per Atkinson et al. 2008.
notes: Dietary fibre is 0.0 g/100 g as reported by the Singapore HPB Foodo database for this gingerbread cookie product; this is a confirmed zero per the approved source, consistent with a refined-flour, no-added-fibre commercial cookie formulation. Calcium is 0.0 mg/100 g and iron is 0.0 mg/100 g as reported by HPB; these are confirmed zero values per the approved source. Vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and moisture data for this specific HPB gingerbread cookie record are not reported in the Singapore HPB Foodo database, and the USDA SR Legacy gingersnap entry does not provide a sufficiently close match for all micronutrients to be adopted without risk of misrepresentation. GI value revised from input record's 'Embedded GI Database' (non-approved, confidence 0.45) to 56 based on analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008. The dietary_tags field in the input included 'plant_based'; this has been removed as the product contains egg and likely dairy (butter), making it neither vegan nor strictly plant-based. Trans fat of 0.1 g/100 g is estimated from USDA SR Legacy gingersnap data and reflects typical commercial cookie formulations using partially hydrogenated shortening; this value should be verified against product-specific labelling if available.
